Title: We Hold These Truths. What the 2026 Record Is Doing to Them.

Title: The Negativity Is Killing You: The Case for Retiring Hatred and Unnecessary Stress
Teaser: Your body does not know the difference between the driver who cut you off and a predator. It responds to both with the same cascade of cortisol, inflammation, and cardiovascular activation. The research across three decades is unambiguous: chronic hatred, sustained anger, and unnecessary stress are physiological events with measurable, cumulative, lethal consequences. The case for retiring them is not moral. It is biological.

Title: The Bonus Army of 1932: When the United States Government Burned Its Own Veterans Out of Washington
Teaser: In the summer of 1932, 43,000 World War I veterans camped outside the Capitol asking for early payment of bonuses the government had promised them. Hoover sent MacArthur, Eisenhower, and Patton to remove them. MacArthur disobeyed two direct presidential orders and burned the camp to the ground. Eisenhower said he was embarrassed by it for the rest of his life.

Title: Neither Columbus Nor George Washington Had a Visa: An American Chronology of Death, Displacement, Theatrical Property Rights and the Historical Meaning of Illegal

The primary source record on who invented the word illegal is more comprehensive than most of these debates reflect.

Title: The United States Would Not Train Bessie Coleman as a Pilot. France Did. She Came Back as the First Internationally Licensed Black Aviator.
Teaser: Bessie Coleman applied to American flight schools in 1919 and was rejected by all of them — some because she was Black, some because she was a woman. She taught herself French, moved to Paris, trained at the Fédération Aéronautique Internationale-certified school, and earned an international pilot's license in 1921. No American — male or female — had done what she did.
Verified Source: Fédération Aéronautique Internationale license records 1921; Chicago Defender coverage 1921-1926; Robert Abbott correspondence (Newberry Library); Coleman's FAI certificate No. 18310

Title: Mary Bowser Worked as a Spy Inside the Confederate White House. The Union's Intelligence Records Document What She Passed On.
Teaser: Mary Bowser worked as an enslaved domestic servant in the Confederate White House in Richmond, Virginia during the Civil War. She was, in fact, a free woman working as a Union spy under Elizabeth Van Lew's network. Postwar Union military records document her contributions. The Confederate government never detected her. Her name was not widely known until postwar memoirs and declassified Union records were examined.
Verified Source: Elizabeth Van Lew memoirs (Library of Congress); Union Military Intelligence records (National Archives); Bowser's addition to US Military Intelligence Hall of Fame 1995; John Babcock intelligence reports

Title: William Leidesdorff Built San Francisco's First Hotel, First Public School, and First Steamboat Line. He Died in 1848. His Estate Was Stolen.
Teaser: William Leidesdorff was California's first Black millionaire. He built San Francisco's first hotel, organized the city's first public school, launched the first steamboat service on San Francisco Bay, and served as US sub-consul to Mexican California. He died in May 1848, weeks after gold was discovered on land he owned near Sutter's Mill. A US Army captain then purchased his entire estate for $75,000 by falsely claiming to be Leidesdorff's heir.
Verified Source: California Land Commission records 1851; San Francisco Probate Court records 1848; 'Alta California' newspaper death notice 1848; US Army Captain Joseph Folsom estate acquisition records

Title: Solomon Linda Wrote 'The Lion Sleeps Tonight' in 1939. The Song Earned Millions. He Died Earning Pennies. His Daughters Sued in 2004.
Teaser: In 1939, South African Zulu musician Solomon Linda recorded 'Mbube' with his group the Evening Birds for Gallo Record Company. He received a one-time payment of ten shillings. The song became 'The Lion Sleeps Tonight' and one of the most-played songs of the 20th century. Linda died in 1962 with an estate of approximately $22. His daughters filed suit in 2004 and reached a settlement with Disney. The copyright registration and court filings are in the public record.
Verified Source: South Africa copyright registration 'Mbube' 1939; Folkways Records US copyright registration; Disney/Abilene Music lawsuit settlement 2006; Rolling Stone 'In the Jungle' by Rian Malan 2000; South African Copyright Act

Title: Ira Aldridge Was the Celebrated Shakespearean Actor of 19th-Century Europe. He Could Not Perform in America.
Teaser: Ira Aldridge was born in New York around 1807. By the 1830s he was performing Othello, Macbeth, and King Lear to standing ovations in London, Berlin, Vienna, and St. Petersburg. The Prussian government awarded him the Medal of Arts and Sciences. The Austrian Emperor received him. He never performed on an American stage.
Verified Source: Royal Coburg Theatre London reviews 1825; Prussian Medal of Arts and Sciences award records; Russian Imperial Theatre records; 'The Times' London reviews 1833; Aldridge Swedish national naturalisation records

Title: John P. Parker Escaped Slavery, Bought His Freedom, Patented Agricultural Inventions, and Ran an Underground Railroad Station — Then Gave One Interview About It
Teaser: John P. Parker was born into slavery in Virginia in 1827, escaped, was recaptured, bought his own freedom, moved to Ripley, Ohio, ran a foundry, received multiple patents for agricultural equipment, and made hundreds of crossings into Kentucky to bring enslaved people to freedom — at documented personal risk each time. In the 1880s he gave one interview to a journalist. The manuscript sat in a drawer at Duke University for a century.
Verified Source: US Patent 385652 (portable screw for tobacco press 1888); Frank Gregg manuscript 1880s (Duke University Rare Book Collection); Ripley Ohio county records; Underground Railroad Network to Freedom (NPS)

Title: Lewis Howard Latimer Drew the Patent Diagrams for Alexander Graham Bell's Telephone and Then Made Thomas Edison's Lightbulb Actually Work
Teaser: Lewis Howard Latimer drafted the patent application drawings for Alexander Graham Bell's telephone in 1876. He then developed and patented the carbon filament process (US Patent 252386) that made Edison's incandescent lightbulb practical and affordable. He wrote the first technical textbook on electric lighting. He worked directly for Edison. He was the only Black member of the Edison Pioneers.
Verified Source: US Patent 252386 (Process of Manufacturing Carbons 1882); Bell patent application 1876 (Latimer's drawings, USPTO); 'Incandescent Electric Lighting: A Practical Description of the Edison System' (1890); Edison Pioneers membership records

Title: Marcelino Serna Was the Most Decorated Mexican-Born Soldier in World War One. When He Came Home, the Army Had to Stop His Deportation.
Teaser: Marcelino Serna was born in Chihuahua, Mexico, crossed into the United States, was drafted into the US Army in 1917 despite not speaking English, refused a transfer to a Spanish-speaking unit, and became the most decorated Mexican-born soldier in World War One. He received the Distinguished Service Cross, two Croix de Guerre, and other honors. When he returned to Texas, he nearly faced deportation as an undocumented immigrant. The Army intervened.
Verified Source: Distinguished Service Cross citation Serna (National Archives); Croix de Guerre citations (French military records); US Army draft registration records 1917; El Paso Times 1918-1919; Texas State Historical Association records

Title: Clifton Wharton Sr. Passed the Foreign Service Exam in 1924. It Took Two More Decades Before the State Department Would Give Him an Ambassadorship.
Teaser: In 1924, Clifton Wharton Sr. became the first African American to pass the US Foreign Service examination. He spent his career in consular posts in the Canary Islands, Madagascar, Portugal, and Romania before becoming the first African American to head a US diplomatic mission to a non-Black-majority country — Bucharest — in 1958. The State Department's own records document the path.
Verified Source: US State Department Foreign Service personnel records; USIA records; 'The Foreign Service Journal' Wharton profile; Department of State historical office records; Clifton Wharton Jr. memoir references

Title: Large Language Models Do Not Know Things. They Predict Text. The Distinction Is Not Semantic — It Changes Everything About How You Use Them.
Teaser: When you ask ChatGPT a question and receive a confident, fluent answer, you are receiving the output of a statistical prediction system, not a retrieval system that found a fact. The technical architecture of large language models was designed to predict the next token in a sequence. It was not designed to know whether that prediction is true.

Title: When Innovation Is a Marketing Word: The Documented Pattern From New Coke to the Metaverse.

Title: Maya Mathematics: The Independent Invention of Zero That Was Centuries Ahead of Europe

Title: Moundville: The Mississippian City That Organized 10,000 People Around a Planned Ceremonial Center
Teaser: Moundville, Alabama was one of the largest pre-Columbian cities in North America, covering 300 acres and housing thousands of people around a planned ceremonial center of 29 platform mounds. Its political organization, craft specialization, and mortuary ritual document a complex chiefdom that functioned as the dominant political center of the middle South for three centuries.

Title: Mesa Verde: The Cliff Palace Engineering That Housed 100 People in the Face of a Canyon Wall
Teaser: Between 1190 and 1280 CE, Ancestral Puebloan builders constructed Cliff Palace — 150 rooms and 23 kivas in the natural alcove of a canyon wall — and over 600 other cliff dwellings across Mesa Verde. The construction required engineering precision and load-bearing calculation that modern architects study as a masterwork of constraint-driven design.

Title: The Inca Road System: 24,000 Miles of Imperial Infrastructure Built Without Wheeled Vehicles or Iron Tools
Teaser: At its maximum extent, the Inca road network stretched 24,000 miles across some of the most difficult terrain on Earth — through Andean passes at 17,000 feet, across coastal deserts, through Amazonian foothills. It was built without wheeled vehicles, iron tools, or draft animals larger than the llama. The Romans built 50,000 miles of roads. The Inca built half that, in terrain the Romans never encountered.

Teaser: The Arctic is the most extreme human environment on Earth. The Inuit developed and refined a complete technological system for surviving it over 5,000 years. Modern cold-weather technology — from polar expedition gear to military arctic equipment — is largely derived from Inuit innovation.

Title: How Many People Were Really Here? The Pre-Columbian Population Debate and What the Evidence Shows
Teaser: Early 20th-century estimates placed the pre-Columbian population of North America at 1-2 million — designed more to justify dispossession than to reflect reality. Current scholarly consensus, based on archaeological survey, agricultural capacity modeling, and disease mortality records, places the figure between 8 and 18 million, with some estimates reaching higher.

Title: The Haudenosaunee Longhouse: How an Architectural Choice Encoded a Constitutional Philosophy
Teaser: The Haudenosaunee Confederacy called itself the Longhouse People because the confederacy's political structure mirrored the longhouse's architecture: multiple family units sharing a common structure with a common fire, governed by the clan mothers who owned the building. The political philosophy encoded in this metaphor is the subject of documented scholarly debate about its influence on the US Constitution.

Title: Chichen Itza's El Castillo: The Building That Functions as a Calendar Twice a Year, to the Day
Teaser: At each spring and fall equinox, a serpent of shadow descends the north staircase of El Castillo at Chichen Itza — a phenomenon produced by the pyramid's precise astronomical alignment. The structure was designed to function as a calendar instrument, encoding the solar year in stone.

Title: Wampum Belts as Treaties: How the Haudenosaunee Encoded Law in Beaded Memory
Teaser: Wampum belts were not currency. They were legal documents — mnemonic records of treaties, agreements, and constitutions, read aloud by trained keepers whose knowledge of the encoded patterns preserved treaty terms across generations. The Two-Row Wampum belt, recording the first treaty between the Haudenosaunee and Dutch colonists, is still cited in Haudenosaunee legal arguments before contemporary courts.

Title: The Great Plains Bison Economy: Indigenous Ecological Management of a Keystone Species at Continental Scale
Teaser: Before European contact, between 30 and 60 million bison roamed the Great Plains in a sustainable ecosystem maintained by thousands of years of Indigenous management. Plains nations used controlled fire, selective hunting, and territorial management to maintain the bison herds that were the foundation of their economy. The management system was deliberately destroyed to subjugate Plains nations in the 1870s.

Title: The Great Law of Peace: The Haudenosaunee Constitution That Preceded and May Have Shaped the US Constitution
Teaser: The Gayanashagowa — the Great Law of Peace — was the constitutional framework of the Haudenosaunee Confederacy, establishing checks on executive power, procedures for impeachment, protections for individual rights, and a federal system of sovereign nations united under common deliberation. The US Senate passed a resolution in 1987 acknowledging its influence on the American constitutional system.

Title: The Great Peace of Montreal 1701: How 39 Indigenous Nations Negotiated a Multi-Party Peace Treaty That Held
Teaser: In August 1701, representatives of 39 Indigenous nations gathered in Montreal and negotiated a multi-party peace agreement with France that ended decades of devastating warfare in the Great Lakes region. The treaty involved more sovereign parties than the Peace of Westphalia (1648), established a framework for ongoing diplomacy, and held as an operative agreement for decades.

Title: Treaty of Fort Laramie 1868: What the Text Actually Says, and What the US Government Did Instead
Teaser: The 1868 Fort Laramie Treaty, ratified by the US Senate, guaranteed the Lakota, Dakota, and Nakota nations permanent ownership of the Black Hills. Six years later, the US government allowed settlers and miners to enter the territory. The Supreme Court ruled in 1980 that the US had illegally seized the Black Hills. The Lakota have refused the settlement payment and continue to seek return of the land.

Title: The Doctrine of Discovery: The 1493 Papal Bull That Became US Law and Has Never Been Fully Repealed
Teaser: The legal basis for European and subsequently American claims to Indigenous lands traces to a 1493 papal bull — Inter Caetera — which granted Spain sovereignty over any lands not previously possessed by any Christian king. This principle was incorporated into US law through the 1823 Supreme Court case Johnson v. M'Intosh. The Vatican repudiated Inter Caetera in 2023. Johnson v. M'Intosh has not been overturned.

Teaser: The Cherokee Nation won two consecutive US Supreme Court cases establishing their sovereignty and invalidating Georgia's extension of state law over Cherokee territory. Title: The Indian Removal Act of 1830: The Legislative History of a Forced Migration
Teaser: The Indian Removal Act of 1830, passed by Congress and signed by President Andrew Jackson, authorized the forced relocation of the Five Civilized Tribes from the southeastern United States to territory west of the Mississippi. The legislative debate was vigorous — opponents called it a violation of treaty obligations and basic justice. It passed anyway.

Title: The Dawes Act of 1887: How Allotment Stripped 90 Million Acres from Indigenous Nations in 50 Years
Teaser: The Dawes Severalty Act of 1887 authorized the federal government to divide communally held tribal lands into individual allotments — 160 acres per family head — with 'surplus' land sold to settlers. In 50 years, Indigenous nations lost 90 million acres — approximately two-thirds of their remaining land base — through the allotment process.

Title: The American Indian Boarding Schools: The Government-Funded System That Explicitly Aimed to Destroy Indigenous Identity
Teaser: Between 1879 and the 1970s, approximately 367 federally funded boarding schools operated in 37 states to forcibly remove Indigenous children from their families and communities. The stated mission, in the words of founder Richard Henry Pratt, was to 'kill the Indian in him, and save the man.' The US Department of the Interior investigated 408 schools in 2022 and confirmed the widespread abuse and death of children.

Title: Native Sovereignty: What Tribal Nations Actually Are Under US Federal Law — and What That Means for Jurisdiction
Teaser: The nearly 600 federally recognized tribal nations in the United States occupy a unique constitutional status: they are sovereign nations with inherent governmental powers, not created by the US government but recognized by it. They predate the Constitution. Their sovereignty is limited by federal law but not extinguished by it — a legal status with significant consequences for taxation, criminal jurisdiction, and self-governance.

Title: The Indian Self-Determination Act of 1975: The Shift From Federal Control to Tribal Governance
Teaser: The Indian Self-Determination and Education Assistance Act of 1975 authorized tribal nations to contract with federal agencies to administer programs on their own reservations — reversing decades of federal control over reservation governance. The policy shift followed the American Indian Movement's occupation of Wounded Knee in 1973 and represented the largest formal expansion of tribal self-governance in the 20th century.

Title: Red Cloud's War: The Only War the US Army Lost and Then Abandoned the Territory
Teaser: Red Cloud's War (1866–1868) is the only conflict in US history in which the government sued for peace, abandoned its military installations, and signed a treaty giving the opposing party what it had demanded. The Lakota, Cheyenne, and Arapaho forces led by Red Cloud closed the Bozeman Trail and burned the US Army's forts. The Fort Laramie Treaty of 1868 acknowledged their victory.

Title: The American Indian Movement: What AIM Was, What It Did, and What the FBI Did in Response

Title: The Indian Child Welfare Act of 1978: Why Congress Had to Pass a Law Against Child Removal
Teaser: By the 1970s, between 25% and 35% of all Native American children were being separated from their families and placed in non-Native foster homes, adoptive homes, or institutions — a rate vastly disproportionate to comparable poverty-level non-Native populations. Congress passed the Indian Child Welfare Act in 1978 to establish minimum procedural protections. It has been under legal challenge since.

Title: Indigenous Astronomy: How Native American Cultures Mapped the Night Sky With Precision That Modern Science Verifies
Teaser: Indigenous North American cultures developed detailed astronomical knowledge — star calendars, tracking of planetary cycles, alignment of ceremonial structures to celestial events — that modern ethnoastronomers have verified through telescope confirmation. The knowledge was transmitted orally across generations, embedded in ceremonial practice, and is still used by contemporary Indigenous communities.

Title: Three Sisters Agriculture: The Nitrogen-Fixing Polyculture System That Fed Eastern North America for 2,000 Years
Teaser: The Three Sisters planting system — corn, beans, and squash grown together in mounds — is one of the most sophisticated polyculture systems ever developed. Corn provides a climbing structure for beans; beans fix atmospheric nitrogen, fertilizing the corn and squash; squash leaves shade the ground to retain moisture and suppress weeds. The system required no synthetic fertilizers and maintained soil productivity for centuries.

Title: Willow Bark to Aspirin: How Indigenous Pain Relief Became the World's Most Common Drug
Teaser: Indigenous peoples across North America, Europe, and Asia used willow bark as a pain reliever for documented millennia before Western pharmacology isolated its active compound (salicin) in 1828 and synthesized aspirin from it in 1897. The traditional knowledge was accurate, effective, and centuries ahead of the scientific explanation.

Title: The Haudenosaunee Public Health System: Governance Structures That Managed Disease Across Multiple Nations
Teaser: The Haudenosaunee Confederacy maintained public health governance structures — including quarantine protocols, food safety standards embedded in longhouse governance, and inter-community health coordination — that managed disease across a multi-nation territory. These systems are documented in the earliest European contact literature and in contemporary Indigenous health research.

Title: Permaculture Before the Word Existed: Indigenous Land Management Systems That Modern Ecology Is Rediscovering
Teaser: Permaculture — the design philosophy of working with rather than against natural systems to produce food and fiber — is often dated to Bill Mollison and David Holmgren's 1978 book. The core principles were practiced by Indigenous peoples across the Americas for millennia: food forests, controlled burns, selective harvesting, and managed succession.

Title: The Corn's Story: How Indigenous Breeding Transformed Teosinte Into the World's Most Produced Grain
Teaser: Modern maize (corn) is so different from its wild ancestor, teosinte, that 20th-century botanists initially doubted they were related. The transformation — from a grass with a few seeds on a small spike to a crop with hundreds of seeds on a large cob — was accomplished by Indigenous plant breeders in Mesoamerica over approximately 9,000 years. Modern corn produces 1.1 billion metric tons per year.

Title: The Pacific Navigation System: How Polynesian Voyagers Crossed the Pacific Without Instruments
Teaser: Polynesian navigators settled Hawaii, New Zealand, Rapa Nui, and virtually every habitable island across 10 million square miles of the Pacific Ocean — the largest migration in human prehistory — using navigation techniques based on stars, waves, wind, currents, clouds, birds, and phosphorescence. The system was preserved in oral tradition and is still practiced by master navigators in the Caroline Islands.

Title: Arctic Pharmacology: The Inuit Medicine System That Managed Nutrition in the World's Most Extreme Diet
Teaser: The traditional Inuit diet — primarily meat and fat with minimal plant food — appears to violate basic nutritional science, yet Inuit populations maintained health on it for 5,000 years. The explanation involves specific food preparation practices that preserve nutrients destroyed by cooking, and animal-source nutrients that substitute for plant-derived vitamins. European explorers who rejected the diet died of scurvy. Inuit populations eating the same diet did not.

Title: Indigenous Water Management in the Arid Southwest: How the Tohono O'odham Farmed the Desert for 2,000 Years
Teaser: The Tohono O'odham and Akimel O'odham peoples of the Sonoran Desert developed and maintained a desert water harvesting system — flash flood irrigation, water spreading fields, and ak-chin farming — that produced reliable harvests in an environment receiving less than 12 inches of rain per year. Modern water engineers study the system as a model for dryland agriculture.

Title: The California Mission System: A Documented Genocide Against a Million People That Textbooks Call 'Conversion'
Teaser: Between 1769 and 1833, the Spanish mission system operated in California reduced the Indigenous population from approximately 300,000 to 100,000 — a 66% decline in 64 years. The missions enforced labor through physical violence, prevented tribal members from leaving, destroyed traditional food systems, and spread epidemic disease in enclosed populations. In 2020, Pope Francis canonized Junipero Serra, who founded the system.

Title: The Osage Reign of Terror: The Documented Murder Campaign Against the Wealthiest People in the World
Teaser: In the 1920s, the Osage Nation of Oklahoma became the wealthiest people per capita in the world when oil was discovered on their land. Within years, Osage people were dying in a systematic murder campaign orchestrated by white men seeking their oil headrights. The FBI's investigation, its first major case, resulted in convictions. The full scope of the killing — estimated at dozens to hundreds of victims — has never been fully prosecuted.

Title: Wounded Knee 1890: What the Army Did, What the Records Say, and Who Got Medals for It
Teaser: On December 29, 1890, soldiers of the US 7th Cavalry surrounded a Lakota encampment at Wounded Knee Creek on the Pine Ridge Reservation and opened fire on Big Foot's band. Between 250 and 300 Lakota were killed — approximately two-thirds women and children. Twenty US soldiers received the Medal of Honor for the engagement. No soldier was charged with any offense. A bill to rescind the medals passed the House in 2024.

Title: Blood Quantum: The Federal Definition of Indianness That Has No Basis in Indigenous Culture
Teaser: Blood quantum — the practice of measuring Indigenous identity by fraction of 'Indian blood' and setting enrollment thresholds at one-quarter, one-eighth, or other fractions — was imposed by the federal government primarily through the Dawes Act era rolls. It has no basis in how Indigenous nations historically defined membership. It functions, over generations, as a mathematical definition of extinction.

Title: Forced Sterilization of Native American Women: The Indian Health Service Program That Sterilized an Estimated 25-50% of Native Women in the 1970s
Teaser: Between 1970 and 1976, the Indian Health Service sterilized an estimated 25-50% of Native American women of childbearing age — using coerced consent, sterilizing minors, and performing procedures under threat of loss of medical care or welfare benefits. The General Accounting Office investigated in 1976 and confirmed 3,406 sterilizations in a four-area sample. The practice was not confined to four areas.

Title: The Missing Treaties: How the US Senate Ratified Some and Buried 18 California Treaties That Would Have Protected 8 Million Acres
Teaser: In 1851-1852, federal commissioners negotiated 18 treaties with California Indigenous nations that would have set aside approximately 8.5 million acres as reservation land. The California legislature petitioned against them. The US Senate held a secret session, voted not to ratify, and ordered the treaties filed under injunction of secrecy. They sat in a Senate vault for 50 years. The land was not reserved.

Title: Hollywood's Indian Problem: How a Century of Film Has Shaped What Americans Think They Know About Native People
Teaser: From the earliest silent films through the 21st century, Hollywood's representation of Native Americans has been characterized by a small number of stock types — the noble savage, the bloodthirsty warrior, the wise elder — performed predominantly by non-Native actors, and embedded in narratives that position Native people as historical rather than contemporary. Research documents measurable psychological harm to Indigenous children from these representations.

Title: NAGPRA: The 1990 Law Requiring Return of Native Remains and Sacred Objects — and What Has Not Been Returned
Teaser: The Native American Graves Protection and Repatriation Act (1990) required museums and federal agencies to inventory and return Native American human remains, funerary objects, and sacred items to lineal descendants and affiliated tribes. After 34 years, more than 110,000 Native American individuals' remains remain in US museum collections — more than were known to be in collections when the law was passed.

Title: Missing and Murdered Indigenous Women: What the Data Shows and Why the Federal Response Has Been Inadequate
Teaser: Native American women are murdered at rates more than 10 times the national average in some areas. Over 5,700 cases of missing and murdered Indigenous women were reported to the National Crime Information Center in 2016 — yet only 116 were logged in DOJ's federal database. The Savanna's Act (2020) and the Not Invisible Act (2020) required improved data collection and coordination. Implementation remains incomplete.

Title: Uranium Mining on Indigenous Lands: The Radioactive Contamination That Federal Agencies Knew About and Did Not Stop
Teaser: Over 500 abandoned uranium mines on Navajo Nation lands were worked by mostly Navajo miners who were not told they were mining radioactive material. The mines produced the uranium for US nuclear weapons. The federal government knew about radiation hazards from the 1940s and did not require protective measures or inform workers until the 1980s. Navajo Nation residents still live near contaminated water sources.

Title: Land Back: The Growing Movement to Return Indigenous Territories — and What Has Actually Happened
Teaser: The Land Back movement calls for the return of Indigenous territories to Indigenous governance and ownership. It is often dismissed as utopian. Recent years have produced documented land return: Timbisha Shoshone land in California, Esselen territory in Big Sur, the Standing Bear's victory in 1879, and hundreds of Canadian land settlements. The question of what 'Land Back' means in practice is being answered in case law and negotiation.

Title: Erasure by Renaming: The 9,000 Derogatory Place Names That Were Changed — and the Thousands That Were Not

Title: The Indian Citizenship Act of 1924: Native Americans Became Citizens of a Country That Was Still Taking Their Land
Teaser: The Indian Citizenship Act of 1924 granted US citizenship to all Native Americans born in the United States. It did not guarantee the right to vote — multiple states continued to prohibit Native Americans from voting through the 1940s and in some cases the 1960s. It also did not affect treaty rights, tribal sovereignty, or the ongoing dispossession of tribal lands through allotment.

Title: The Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act: How Corporate Structure Replaced Indigenous Land Rights — and What It Did to the Culture
Teaser: The Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act of 1971 extinguished 300 million acres of Aboriginal title claims for $962 million and 44 million acres, distributing the settlement through 12 regional corporations and 200 village corporations in which Alaska Natives received shares. Title: The Population That Survived: Native American Demographic Recovery After the Nadir
Teaser: The Native American population reached a historic low of approximately 237,000 in the 1900 census — down from pre-contact estimates of 5-18 million. By 2020, the Census counted 9.7 million Americans identifying as Native American alone or in combination. The recovery is not a return to pre-contact conditions — it is the story of a people who did not disappear despite policies designed to make them do so.

Title: We Binge on Clown News So You Do Not Have To. Facts Matter More Than Ever. This Is Why.
Teaser: Modern American journalism has been reconfigured around a single variable: which demographic will pay the most to have its existing beliefs confirmed. The documented result is a media landscape that is simultaneously more racially pandered to and less racially diverse in its newsrooms than at any point in recent memory. ClownBinge was built as the answer to that specific, documented failure. This is the founding record.
